weather.current_meta: Feels like 26°C · Low 21°C · Rain 0% · Gusts 13 mph · Humidity 72% · peak ~36°C today

warnings.title: Extreme heat — South East England (Met Office)
warnings.body_html: Red Extreme Heat Warning in force for the South East, including Surrey, until 21:00 Thu 25 June. The Met Office said Thursday is the peak of the heat, with up to 38°C possible in the South East; it follows a provisional UK June record of about 36°C on Wednesday. An Amber extreme-heat warning then extends into Friday 26 and Saturday 27 June before the heat breaks down.

warnings.title: Heat-health alert — UKHSA
warnings.body_html: UKHSA Red heat-health alert covering South East England until 23:00 Thu 25 June. The agency says hot weather is likely to affect people of all ages, with increased risk to life across the population and added pressure on health and social-care services. It is only the second Red heat-health alert UKHSA has issued.

days.sum: Peak of the heatwave near 36°C, feels like 38°C; Red warning until 21:00.
Still hot and humid at 34°C with a small late thunder risk; Amber warning.
Heat breaks down to 29°C with thundery showers possible and a fresher feel.
Much cooler and more comfortable at 24°C with a passing shower.
Pleasant, near-average late-June temperatures at 23°C; mostly dry.
Settled and light winds at 23°C, with a few clouds bubbling up.
Warm with sunny spells at 24°C and an isolated light shower possible.

sources.title: Red extreme heat warning in force as record-breaking June temperatures forecast

hero.headline: UK provisionally records its hottest June day as red heat warnings hit western Europe
hero.summary: The Met Office said the UK reached about 36°C on 24 June, provisionally its highest June temperature on record, as red extreme-heat warnings covered Surrey and South-East England and parts of France and Italy issued their own alerts.

cards.headline: US and Iran agree Hormuz safe-passage line; IMO to evacuate 11,000 seafarers
cards.summary: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran agreed a communication line for safe shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and a Lebanon "de-confliction cell," as the IMO announced an evacuation of more than 11,000 stranded seafarers.

cards.headline: Trump cancels housing-bill signing, tying it to an elections bill
cards.summary: Hours before a planned ceremony, Trump said he would not sign the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act until Congress passes the SAVE America Act on voter registration.

cards.headline: Wall Street steadies on rotation out of tech; Micron jumps after the close on earnings
cards.summary: The Dow rose and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq finished little changed on 24 June as investors rotated out of chips, while oil and gold fell on easing Middle East tension; Micron rose about 15% in after-hours trading.

cards.headline: Appeals court lets Trump administration resume nationwide expedited removal
cards.summary: A divided D.C. Circuit panel vacated a lower-court order that had blocked fast-track deportation without a hearing for people who cannot prove two years' continuous presence.

cards.headline: Ukraine strikes occupied Crimea; officials report widespread power loss
cards.summary: Ukraine said it destroyed a railway bridge over the North Crimean Canal and hit energy and fuel sites overnight; occupation authorities reported about half the peninsula lost power.

cards.headline: Micron beats estimates and guides higher; shares jump after the Anthropic AI-memory deal
cards.summary: Micron reported fiscal Q3 revenue of about $41.5bn and adjusted EPS of $25.11, guided to roughly $50bn for the current quarter, and rose about 15% after the close, days after its multi-year supply deal with Anthropic.

cards.headline: Study identifies why H5N1 infects dairy cows' udders rather than their lungs
cards.summary: University of Pittsburgh researchers found H5N1 binds N-linked sialic acid receptors abundant in udder tissue but scarce in cow airways.

cards.headline: SETI radio search of interstellar object 3I/ATLAS finds no technosignatures
cards.summary: A SETI Institute-led scan detected no artificial radio signals, with all candidate detections attributed to human-made interference.

cards.headline: World Cup: Scotland beaten 2-0 by Brazil and exit; England held by Ghana
cards.summary: Vinícius Júnior scored twice as Brazil beat Scotland 2-0 in Miami on 24 June, ending Scotland's bid for a first World Cup knockout place, a day after England were held 0-0 by Ghana.

stories.headline: UK provisionally records its hottest June day as red heat warnings hit western Europe
stories.summary: The Met Office said the UK reached about 36°C on 24 June, provisionally its highest June temperature on record, as red extreme-heat warnings covered Surrey and South-East England and parts of France and Italy issued their own alerts.

confidence.label: Developing
confidence.why: — the Met Office record and the red warnings are confirmed by the Met Office, UKHSA and multiple outlets; the headline temperature is provisional and individual station figures are subject to verification

image.alt: The Sun photographed in white light, showing sunspots and limb darkening
image.caption: The Sun in white light. Western Europe recorded extreme temperatures on 24 June 2026 under clear skies and a high-pressure system. Photo: Matúš Motlo / Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0

inline_image.alt: Dry, cracked ground in an arid landscape
inline_image.caption: Dry, cracked ground in the Sonoran Desert. Illustrative; the Met Office issued a Red Extreme Heat Warning for parts of England on 24–25 June. Photo: Tomas Castelazo / Wikimedia Commons · CC BY 3.0

article_blocks.html: The United Kingdom provisionally recorded its hottest June day on Wednesday, 24 June 2026, the Met Office said, as a Red Extreme Heat Warning covered parts of southern England and red alerts were issued in several other western European countries. The agency said the heat would peak on Thursday, 25 June, with the red warning remaining in force into Thursday evening.

The Met Office said a provisional high of about 36°C was reached at Wisley in Surrey and at Gosport in Hampshire. The previous June record had been passed earlier in the day, first at Charlwood in Surrey and then at Wiggonholt in West Sussex, the agency said. The figures are provisional and subject to quality control before they are confirmed.

A Red Extreme Heat Warning — the Met Office's highest heat warning level — was in force for parts of central, southern and eastern England, including Surrey, from 09:00 on 24 June to 21:00 on 25 June. The Met Office said it was only the second red extreme-heat warning it had issued, and that temperatures could reach 37–40°C in places on Thursday.

The UK Health Security Agency issued a Red heat-health alert for South-East England and several other regions, in force from 01:00 on 24 June to 23:00 on 25 June. The agency said the heat was likely to affect people of all ages and to increase pressure on health and social-care services. UKHSA has issued only one previous red heat-health alert, in July 2022.

Forecasters attributed the heat to a high-pressure system drawing hot air northward from continental Europe and north Africa, combined with clear skies and high June sun. Overnight temperatures in parts of the South East were forecast to stay above 20°C, conditions the Met Office described as "tropical nights."

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the June 2026 heat
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 21 Jun
events.title: Heat builds
events.detail: High pressure draws hot air north over western Europe; warnings are prepared.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Red alerts on the continent
events.detail: France places about two-thirds of departments under red alert; a high of 44.3°C is reported at Pissos, in the Landes.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: UK June record
events.detail: The Met Office reports a provisional 36°C at Wisley and Gosport; a Red Extreme Heat Warning is in force.

events.label: 25 Jun
events.title: Peak forecast
events.detail: The Met Office says 37–40°C is possible before the heat breaks down later in the week.

article_blocks.text: "Temperatures will continue to climb through the week, with the potential for the June temperature record to be broken."

article_blocks.html: Elsewhere in Europe, France's national weather service, Météo-France, placed a large share of the country under its highest heat alert during the week, and a temperature of 44.3°C was reported at Pissos, in the Landes, on 23 June. Italy issued red heat alerts for a number of cities, including Rome and Milan, and authorities across the region warned of risks to health and to outdoor workers.

Why it matters: the Met Office and UKHSA said extreme heat raises the risk of illness and death, particularly among older people and those with existing health conditions, and can disrupt transport, energy and water services. Scientists have said that heatwaves are becoming more frequent and intense as the global climate warms; attribution of any single event requires separate study.

What comes next: the Met Office said the heat was expected to peak on Thursday before breaking down from the west later in the week, with thundery showers possible as cooler air moves in. Confirmed record figures and any health impacts were expected to be assessed in the days afterwards.

items: The Met Office said the UK provisionally recorded its hottest June day on 24 June 2026, about 36°C at Wisley (Surrey) and Gosport (Hampshire).
A Red Extreme Heat Warning covered parts of England, including Surrey, from 09:00 on 24 June to 21:00 on 25 June — only the second such warning the Met Office has issued.
UKHSA issued a Red heat-health alert for South-East England (01:00 24 June – 23:00 25 June), its second ever after July 2022.
The Met Office said 37–40°C was possible on 25 June before the heat breaks down.
France reported 44.3°C at Pissos on 23 June; Italy issued red alerts for cities including Rome and Milan.

items.title: Red extreme heat warning in force as record-breaking June temperatures forecast
UK braced for record-breaking 40C as red weather warnings come into force
UKHSA issues red heat-health alerts across England
Red heat warning issued for Sussex and Surrey
2026 European heatwaves

items.text: A Red Extreme Heat Warning (Met Office) and a Red heat-health alert (UKHSA) were in force for parts of England on 24–25 June 2026.
The Met Office described 24 June as provisionally the UK's hottest June day, with about 36°C in Surrey and Hampshire.
Individual station figures (Wisley/Gosport 36°C; Pissos 44.3°C) are provisional and pending confirmation by the respective national services.

agree: Western Europe experienced extreme heat in the week of 24 June 2026.
The Met Office issued a Red Extreme Heat Warning and UKHSA a Red heat-health alert.
The UK provisionally set a June temperature record.
contest: The exact peak figures, which remain provisional.
The degree to which any single heatwave can be attributed to climate change without separate study.

items: 1 · Provisional vs confirmed. Record temperatures are provisional until quality-controlled; the "hottest June day" claim is attributed to the Met Office and may be revised.

2 · Warning level vs measured heat. The Red warning reflects assessed risk to health and infrastructure across a region, not a single measured temperature; the 37–40°C figures are forecasts for 25 June, not recorded values.
3 · Event vs trend. A hot June day is weather; statements about heatwaves becoming more frequent describe a longer-term trend and are kept separate from this single event.

stories.headline: US and Iran agree Hormuz safe-passage line; IMO to evacuate 11,000 seafarers
stories.summary: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran agreed a communication line for safe shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and a Lebanon "de-confliction cell," as the IMO announced an evacuation of more than 11,000 stranded seafarers.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the de-confliction cell, the Hormuz line and the IMO evacuation are each reported by two or more independent outlets plus the IMO statement; some operational details remain thin

image.alt: An oil tanker loading crude oil at an offshore terminal in the Persian Gulf
image.caption: An oil tanker takes on crude oil in the Persian Gulf; about a fifth to a quarter of seaborne oil normally transits the nearby Strait of Hormuz. Photo: U.S. Navy · public domain
image.credit: U.S. Navy photo by Photographer's Mate Airman Eben Boothby

inline_image.alt: The headquarters of the International Maritime Organization on the River Thames in London
inline_image.caption: IMO headquarters in London; the UN agency announced a plan to evacuate more than 11,000 stranded seafarers. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0

article_blocks.html: The United States and Iran agreed to establish a communication line intended to ensure the safe passage of commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz and a "de-confliction cell" covering Lebanon, mediators Qatar and Pakistan said following talks in Switzerland. The arrangements were set out in a joint statement issued by the two mediating states after the negotiating round near Lake Lucerne on 21 June 2026.

According to the mediators, the Hormuz channel is intended "to avoid incidents and miscommunication with the aim of safe passage for commercial vessels," operating within a 60-day window outlined in a US–Iran road map toward a final agreement. The same statement described a de-confliction cell involving the United States, Iran and Lebanon, facilitated by Qatar and Pakistan, to support "the termination of military operations in Lebanon."

Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, called the Lebanon de-confliction cell the "first real test" of the process that emerged from the Switzerland talks. US Vice-President JD Vance said the discussions had set a "good foundation," telling reporters on 22 June 2026: "The final deal is the house. We set the foundation. We haven't built the house, but we've laid a successful foundation."

Separately, the International Maritime Organization announced on 23 June 2026 a plan to evacuate about 11,000 seafarers who remain stranded in the region. The UN agency said the operation would be carried out with Iran, Oman, other coastal states, the United States and the maritime industry.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — Hormuz and the talks
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 28 Feb
events.title: Air war begins
events.detail: US and Israeli strikes on Iran disrupt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

events.label: 4 Mar
events.title: Strait declared closed
events.detail: Iran announces the Strait of Hormuz is "closed" and warns vessels against passage.

events.label: 21 Jun
events.title: Switzerland talks
events.detail: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan report a road map, a Hormuz communication line and a Lebanon de-confliction cell.

events.label: 22 Jun
events.title: Vance comments
events.detail: JD Vance says the talks set a "good foundation" for a final deal.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: IMO evacuation
events.detail: The IMO announces a plan to evacuate more than 11,000 stranded seafarers.

article_blocks.html: IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez said the agency had "secured the necessary safety guarantees and have thoroughly verified the conditions for safe navigation." The IMO said two temporary maritime corridors had been designated to help vessels avoid floating mines and exit the region, with routing communicated through a Notice to Mariners issued by Oman. The agency said 14 seafarers had died during the conflict.

article_blocks.text: "We have secured the necessary safety guarantees and have thoroughly verified the conditions for safe navigation."

article_blocks.html: Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has been largely halted since late February 2026, after US and Israeli airstrikes on Iran; Iran declared the strait "closed" on 4 March 2026, leaving large numbers of vessels and crews in the Gulf.

article_blocks.overview: Coverage overview: reporting is broadly factual and convergent across wire and broadcast outlets, drawing on the mediators' joint statement and the IMO release; differences are mainly of emphasis.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: NPR/CNN Centre, Al Jazeera Lean Left, ABC News Centre; IMO is a primary source.
lines: Left-leaning outlets: stress the humanitarian toll on seafarers and conditions tied to an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon.
Centre/wire: relay the mediators' and IMO statements and the 60-day road map plainly.
Right-leaning outlets: foreground Vance's "good foundation" framing and US negotiating leverage.

article_blocks.html: Why it matters: about a fifth to a quarter of seaborne oil normally transits the Strait of Hormuz. A functioning safe-passage mechanism and a crew evacuation would address both the situation of stranded mariners and a months-long disruption to energy shipping.

What comes next: mediators said a high-level committee approved a road map aimed at a final deal within 60 days, with technical talks on the nuclear file, sanctions and dispute resolution to follow. Lebanon was reported to be pursuing direct talks with Israel; Hezbollah has set a full Israeli withdrawal as a condition. Implementation of the Hormuz line and the pace of the evacuation remain to be confirmed.

items: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran agreed a communication line for safe passage of commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, tied to a 60-day window.
A "de-confliction cell" involving the US, Iran and Lebanon was created to support the end of military operations in Lebanon.
The IMO announced (23 June) an evacuation plan for about 11,000 stranded seafarers, coordinated with Iran, Oman, the US and others.
The IMO designated two temporary corridors and said 14 seafarers died during the conflict.
Iran declared the strait "closed" on 4 March 2026; about a fifth to a quarter of seaborne oil normally transits Hormuz.

items.title: US and Iran agree to a 'road map' for a final deal, mediators say
Iran and US make 'encouraging progress' in talks, mediators say
IMO announces evacuation plan in the Strait of Hormuz
Lebanon discusses 'de-confliction' mechanism ahead of Israel talks
Vance says talks set 'good foundation' for a deal to end the war

items.text: Mediators stated a Hormuz communication line for commercial vessels and a Lebanon de-confliction cell (NPR, CNN, Al Jazeera).
The IMO announced a plan to evacuate about 11,000 seafarers, citing 14 deaths and two temporary corridors (IMO statement, Gulf News).
JD Vance said the talks set a "good foundation" on 22 June 2026 (ABC News, NBC News).
The exact number of vessels still trapped and the precise coordinates of the two corridors; reporting gives "hundreds of vessels" without a confirmed current count.

agree: A Hormuz communication line for commercial shipping was announced.
A Lebanon de-confliction cell was created.
The IMO is moving to evacuate about 11,000 seafarers.
Mediators framed the talks as a 60-day road map.
contest: Whether the mechanisms will hold and end the war.
Hezbollah's demand for a full Israeli withdrawal as a condition.
Characterisations of "progress" against unresolved nuclear-inspection disputes.

items: 1 · Stated vs implemented. The de-confliction cell and safe-passage line are sourced to a mediators' joint statement; they describe stated agreements, not verified on-the-ground implementation.
2 · "Underway" vs "announced." Some outlets said the evacuation is underway while the IMO wording describes a plan; treat operational status as developing.
3 · Counts vary. Earlier reports cited larger vessel and crew figures; avoid conflating cumulative and current numbers with the ~11,000 figure.

stories.headline: Trump cancels housing-bill signing, conditioning it on an elections bill
stories.summary: Hours before a planned ceremony, President Trump said he would not sign the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act until Congress passes the SAVE America Act on voter registration.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the cancelled signing and Trump's statement are confirmed by CBS, CNN, NPR and HousingWire; the underlying vote counts and provisions are corroborated across NPR, NBC, CNBC and TIME

image.alt: The west front of the United States Capitol building in Washington, D.C.
image.caption: The U.S. Capitol, where the House passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act. Photo: Architect of the Capitol · public domain
image.credit: Architect of the Capitol via Wikimedia Commons

inline_image.alt: Official U.S. Senate portrait of Senator Tim Scott
inline_image.caption: Sen. Tim Scott (R-S.C.), Banking Committee chairman and a lead sponsor of the bill. Photo: U.S. Senate · public domain

article_blocks.html: President Donald Trump on 24 June 2026 cancelled a planned ceremony to sign the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, saying he would not sign the bipartisan housing bill until Congress passed a separate elections measure. The announcement came less than two hours before a scheduled news conference and signing, according to CBS News and CNN.

In a post on social media, Trump said: "Today's Housing News Conference and Signing is hereby cancelled until such time as we pass the desperately needed SAVE AMERICA ACT, which I consider to be a National Emergency." The SAVE America Act is a Republican-backed elections bill that would impose new requirements on voter registration and ballot casting. Trump later declined to say whether he would veto the housing bill, according to CNN.

The U.S. House of Representatives had passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act by a vote of 358-32, sending the bill to the president. All 32 votes against were cast by Republicans, while Democrats present voted in favour, according to NPR and NBC News.

The Senate cleared the same measure by 85-5. The five senators who voted against were all Republicans: Ron Johnson of Wisconsin, Mike Lee of Utah, Rand Paul of Kentucky, Rick Scott of Florida and Tommy Tuberville of Alabama, according to TIME. Among the 85 votes in favour were 41 Republicans and 44 Democrats.

"ROAD" stands for "Renewing Opportunity in the American Dream." The legislation was developed by Senate Banking Committee chairman Tim Scott (R-S.C.) and ranking member Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.), with House Financial Services Committee chairman French Hill (R-Ark.) and ranking member Maxine Waters (D-Calif.).

The bill includes more than 45 provisions. A section titled "Homes Are For People, Not Corporations" restricts purchases of single-family homes by large institutional investors that directly or indirectly own at least 350 single-family homes, with exemptions for investors building or buying new homes for the rental market, according to the Bipartisan Policy Center.

Other provisions include a $200 million annual competitive grant program for local governments and tribes, an FHA pilot for mortgages under $100,000, an expansion of the Rental Assistance Demonstration program cap by 100,000 units, and streamlined federal environmental reviews for housing. NBC News reported the package gives local governments incentives to revise zoning rules to allow more homebuilding.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — the housing bill
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.
article_blocks.note: Outlets differ by a day on the exact vote dates.

events.label: Oct 2025
events.title: Bill text released
events.detail: Scott and Warren release the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act package.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Senate passes
events.detail: The Senate votes 85-5 to pass the bill.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: House passes
events.detail: The House votes 358-32, sending the bill to the president.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Signing cancelled
events.detail: Trump cancels the signing ceremony, conditioning his signature on passage of the SAVE America Act.

article_blocks.text: "Every time every member of Congress goes back home they hear how urgent it is to bring down home prices. And that's what the bill does."

article_blocks.overview: Coverage overview: reporting across the spectrum is broadly factual, emphasising the bipartisan vote and the investor provision; outlets differ mainly in adjectives and in framing the result as the president's win or Congress's.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: NPR Centre, NBC/TIME Lean Left, Washington Times Lean Right; Bipartisan Policy Center is non-partisan.
lines: Left-leaning outlets: frame it as a Warren-led consumer-protection and renter measure.
Centre/wire: report vote counts, provisions and timeline with limited characterisation.
Right-leaning outlets: frame it as a Trump and Tim Scott affordability measure and note Republican dissent over federal overreach.

article_blocks.html: Why it matters: NPR described the measure as the largest housing affordability bill in decades. Rep. French Hill called it "one of the most significant bipartisan housing reforms in recent memory." The bill passed during a period of elevated U.S. home prices and constrained supply. A stricter Senate provision that would have required investors to sell newly built homes within seven years was not included, according to NBC News.

What comes next: the bill has passed both chambers but cannot take effect until the president signs it or his veto is overridden; Trump has tied his signature to passage of the SAVE America Act. Trump's earlier proposal to limit large institutional investors from buying single-family homes was incorporated into the final housing text, according to NBC News. The bill's sponsors had described it as the most significant bipartisan housing legislation in years.

items: Trump cancelled the signing ceremony on 24 June, saying he will not sign until the SAVE America Act passes.
House vote: 358-32; all 32 no votes were Republicans.
Senate vote: 85-5; the five no votes were all Republicans (Johnson, Lee, Paul, R. Scott, Tuberville).
Investor limit applies to firms owning at least 350 single-family homes.
The package contains more than 45 provisions, including a $200 million/year grant program.
Lead sponsors: Sens. Tim Scott and Elizabeth Warren; Reps. French Hill and Maxine Waters.

items.title: Trump cancels housing bill signing, ties it to elections legislation

items.text: Trump cancelled the 24 June signing ceremony and conditioned his signature on the SAVE America Act, in a statement quoted by CBS News, CNN and NPR.
House passed 358-32 and Senate 85-5; corroborated by NPR, NBC, TIME and CNBC.
The five GOP no votes were Johnson, Lee, Paul, Rick Scott and Tuberville (TIME).
Whether and when Trump will ultimately sign or veto the bill is unresolved.

agree: The bill passed both chambers with large bipartisan majorities.
It limits institutional investors owning 350+ single-family homes.
It aims to raise supply and lower costs and now heads to the president.
contest: Whether the investor limit and regulatory changes will meaningfully lower prices.
Whether federal action or local zoning is the right lever.

items: 1 · Superlative check. "First major housing legislation since 2008" is stronger than the sourcing; outlets support "largest/most significant in decades" — attribute carefully.
2 · Date check. Passage dates differ by a day between outlets; the official Congress.gov roll-call records would settle the exact calendar dates.

stories.headline: Appeals court lets Trump administration resume nationwide expedited removal
stories.summary: A divided D.C. Circuit panel vacated a lower-court order that had blocked fast-track deportation without a hearing for people who cannot prove two years' continuous presence.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the court, 2-1 vote, panel judges, district judge and policy mechanics are corroborated across NPR/AP, CBS, Fox News and CNN; the exact docket number was not located

image.alt: Exterior of the E. Barrett Prettyman Federal Courthouse in Washington, D.C.
image.caption: The E. Barrett Prettyman U.S. Courthouse in Washington, D.C., which houses the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · public domain

inline_image.alt: Section of the border barrier between Tijuana, Mexico and San Diego, United States
inline_image.caption: The U.S.–Mexico border barrier near Tijuana and San Diego; expedited removal was previously applied mainly at or near the border. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0

article_blocks.html: A three-judge panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit ruled on Tuesday, 23 June 2026, that the Trump administration may resume applying expedited removal nationwide, vacating a lower-court order that had blocked the expansion.

The decision was 2-1. Judge Justin R. Walker, an appointee of President Donald Trump, wrote the majority opinion and was joined by Judge Neomi Rao, also a Trump appointee. Judge Robert Wilkins, an appointee of President Barack Obama, dissented.

Expedited removal is a process under federal immigration law that allows the Department of Homeland Security to deport certain people without a hearing before an immigration judge. It had been applied mainly to people arriving by sea or apprehended at or near the border shortly after crossing. The administration moved in January 2026 to apply it throughout the country to people who cannot show they have lived in the United States continuously for at least two years.

The expansion had been blocked by U.S. District Judge Jia Cobb, an appointee of President Joe Biden, who ruled in August 2025 that the administration had not established procedures to ensure people were not wrongly removed. The appeals panel's majority concluded that challengers were unlikely to succeed on their claim that the policy violates the due-process protections of the Fifth Amendment.

Writing for the majority, Walker said the constitutional requirement is "notice of the action the government is taking and the grounds for it, plus an opportunity to respond," and not that the government "explain how the individual might prevail." The majority held that federal law permits DHS to apply expedited removal as broadly as Congress authorized.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — expedited removal
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.
article_blocks.note: The August 2025 order date is approximate.

events.label: Aug 2025
events.title: District court blocks expansion
events.detail: Judge Jia Cobb halts nationwide expedited removal, citing inadequate safeguards.

events.label: Jan 2026
events.title: Administration expands policy
events.detail: DHS moves to apply expedited removal nationwide to those who cannot prove two-plus years' presence.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Appeals court vacates block
events.detail: A D.C. Circuit panel rules 2-1 for the administration; DHS and the ACLU respond the same day.

article_blocks.text: "The Trump administration's push for fast-track deportations will subject people to an unfair and error-prone system."
article_blocks.src: — Anand Balakrishnan, ACLU Immigrants' Rights Project, 23 June 2026

article_blocks.overview: Coverage overview: outlets across the spectrum converge on the core facts and diverge in tone — right-leaning outlets frame it as a victory and stress the overruled Biden-appointed judge, while left-leaning outlets foreground due-process concerns.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: CNN/CBS Lean Left, NPR/AP Centre/Wire, Fox News/Washington Times Lean Right.
lines: Left-leaning framing: emphasises the risk of wrongful deportation and erosion of due process for long-term residents.
Right-leaning framing: emphasises the court restoring an enforcement tool and reversing a lower-court judge.

article_blocks.html: The Justice Department had argued that Judge Cobb's order was an "egregious error" that deprived the government of an "essential tool" for immigration enforcement. DHS general counsel James Percival said on 23 June 2026 that the court "vindicated our decision to apply the law as written."

What comes next: the American Civil Liberties Union, which represents plaintiffs through its Immigrants' Rights Project, said the decision "undermines the fundamental principle that people receive due process when the government seeks to deport them." In dissent, Judge Wilkins argued that DHS's procedures do not give people a meaningful chance to prove two years' residence. Further litigation is expected.

items: Court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit; vote 2-1, ruling dated 23 June 2026.
Majority: Judges Justin R. Walker and Neomi Rao (Trump appointees); dissent: Judge Robert Wilkins (Obama appointee).
Blocked below by U.S. District Judge Jia Cobb (Biden appointee) in August 2025.
The policy applies expedited removal nationwide to people who cannot prove at least two years' continuous U.S. presence, bypassing an immigration-judge hearing.
The administration expanded the practice in January 2026 from its prior use mainly at or near the border.

items.title: Appeals court allows Trump administration to resume speedy deportations
Federal appeals court allows expanded use of speedy deportations
Federal court allows ICE to expand expedited deportations nationwide
Appeals court revives Trump expedited removal policy
Trump effort to expand speedy deportations can proceed, court rules

items.text: A 2-1 D.C. Circuit panel (Walker and Rao in the majority, Wilkins dissenting) vacated the lower-court block on 23 June 2026.
District Judge Jia Cobb, a Biden appointee, had blocked the nationwide expansion in August 2025.
The policy lets DHS deport people found anywhere in the U.S. without an immigration-judge hearing if they cannot prove two years' continuous presence.
The exact case caption/docket number and the precise date of Judge Cobb's order were not located in the public sources reviewed.

agree: The D.C. Circuit ruled 2-1 to let the administration resume nationwide expedited removal.
The case turns on Fifth Amendment due process.
contest: Whether the procedures adequately protect people who may have lived in the U.S. for two or more years.
Whether the expansion is lawful enforcement or an unfair removal process.

items: 1 · Preliminary posture. The ruling addresses likelihood of success on appeal, not a final merits judgment; calling it a final settlement of the policy's legality would overstate it.
2 · Burden shift. "Cannot prove two years' presence" places the burden on the individual; the dissent disputes whether the process to meet that burden is adequate.
3 · Appointer labels. Naming the appointing presidents describes the judges, not evidence that outcomes were predetermined.

stories.headline: Ukraine strikes occupied Crimea; occupation officials report widespread power loss
stories.summary: Ukraine said it destroyed a railway bridge over the North Crimean Canal and hit energy, fuel and military sites overnight on 23 June; occupation authorities reported about half the peninsula lost power.

confidence.label: Developing
confidence.why: — the bridge strike and Putin's response are confirmed, but several figures (the "half of Crimea" power claim, target counts, the sea-drone account) rest on single sources per side and are attributed

image.alt: Map of the Crimean peninsula and Sevastopol
image.caption: Map of Crimea, the Russian-occupied peninsula where Ukraine reported strikes on 23 June 2026. Illustrative. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC0

inline_image.alt: The North Crimean Canal running through open terrain
inline_image.caption: The North Crimean Canal, over which Ukraine said it destroyed a railway bridge near Rozdolne. Illustrative; not the strike site. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: Ukraine's Special Operations Forces said on 23 June 2026 that they had destroyed a railway bridge over the North Crimean Canal near Rozdolne in occupied Crimea, part of what Kyiv described as a campaign to disrupt Russian logistics on the peninsula. In a statement posted to social media, the force said "the railway bridge across the North Crimean Canal in Crimea no longer exists."

Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces said drones struck more than 60 Russian military targets across occupied territory overnight. Targets cited by Ukrainian sources included fuel reservoirs at the Kerch thermal power plant, an electrical substation near Karierne, a Simferopol gas distribution station, the Port Kavkaz oil terminal, and air-defence equipment. These target lists come from Ukrainian military statements and have not been independently confirmed.

Occupation authority spokesperson Oleg Kryuchkov said about half of Crimea was left without electricity following the strikes, and that rolling blackouts had been introduced. The "half of Crimea" figure originates with occupation officials and could not be independently verified. The local energy supplier in some statements attributed outages to "technical malfunctions" rather than to strikes.
Reporting also noted suspensions of gasoline sales to civilians in parts of Crimea during the summer tourist season, which Ukrainian and Western outlets linked to the strike campaign.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — strikes on Crimea
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 19 Jun
events.title: Zelensky ultimatum to Belarus
events.detail: Kyiv gives Minsk about one week to remove relay equipment it says guides Russian drones.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Rail bridge struck
events.detail: Ukrainian forces say the North Crimean Canal railway bridge near Rozdolne "no longer exists."

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Wider strike campaign
events.detail: Ukraine reports hitting 60+ targets; occupation officials report about half of Crimea without power.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Putin responds
events.detail: Putin orders the consequences of Ukrainian strikes "reduced to zero," per TASS and RIA.

article_blocks.text: "We are seeing almost daily attacks on Crimea's energy infrastructure."
article_blocks.src: — Oleg Kryuchkov, occupation authority spokesperson, 23 June 2026

article_blocks.html: President Vladimir Putin commented on the attacks during a government video conference, in remarks reported by the Russian state agencies TASS and RIA Novosti. Putin said the government "must also take additional measures to minimise, to reduce to zero, the consequences of such actions," and said Ukraine was seeking to disrupt Crimea's tourist season. Ukrainska Pravda noted these were Putin's first public comments on Ukrainian long-range strikes since attacks on the Moscow oil refinery on 16 and 18 June.

Separately, Ukraine said Russia attempted a strike on Ukraine's south-western Black Sea coast early on 23 June using unmanned surface vessels. Defence Ministry adviser Serhii Beskrestnov said the boats were launched at around 4 a.m., were destroyed before reaching the coast, and were fitted with Starlink terminals. This account rests on a single Ukrainian official and is not independently verified.

article_blocks.overview: Coverage overview: accounts split between Ukrainian and Western framing of a deliberate campaign to isolate Crimea and Russian and occupation framing that minimises the impact and attributes outages to technical faults.
article_blocks.caption: Share of the gathered sources by lean - an approximate read, not a measure of the entire press.
article_blocks.ratings_html: Source leans: Reuters/AP Wire, Kyiv Independent/Kyiv Post Pro-Ukraine, TASS/RIA Russian state.
lines: Ukrainian framing: the strikes are precise, lawful targeting of military logistics aimed at making the occupation untenable.
Russian state framing: Ukraine targets civilian tourism and energy to destabilise, with consequences to be "reduced to zero."

article_blocks.html: The Moscow-installed governor of Sevastopol, Mikhail Razvozhayev, said on 24 June that two people had been injured, and Russian and occupation channels reported a fire at the Simferopol thermal power plant and the suspension of public fuel sales in parts of the peninsula. These accounts come from occupation officials and could not be independently verified.

What comes next: the Institute for the Study of War assessed on 23 June that Ukraine is conducting a campaign to limit Russia's ability to move fuel and supplies across the Kerch Strait. The full extent of casualties and the duration of damage to the bridge, substations and fuel sites could not be independently established, and the timing of power restoration was not confirmed by evening.

items: Ukraine said it destroyed a railway bridge over the North Crimean Canal near Rozdolne on 23 June 2026.
Ukrainian forces reported striking more than 60 Russian military targets across occupied territory overnight.
Occupation spokesperson Oleg Kryuchkov said about half of Crimea lost electricity; rolling blackouts were introduced.
Putin on 23 June ordered the consequences of the strikes "reduced to zero" (TASS/RIA Novosti).
Ukraine said it destroyed Russian Starlink-equipped sea drones off its south-western coast early on 23 June.

items.title: Ukraine says it hit a railway bridge to Crimea
Half of Crimea without electricity after Ukrainian strikes, Russian proxy claims
Putin comments on Ukrainian strikes on Crimea
Explained: bridge destroyed, blackouts, fires, fuel bans in Crimea
ISW Russian Offensive Campaign Assessment, June 23, 2026

items.text: Ukraine's Special Operations Forces said it destroyed the North Crimean Canal railway bridge near Rozdolne on 23 June (corroborated by AP/Reuters and Kyiv Post).
Putin said the government must "reduce to zero" the consequences of Ukrainian strikes, per TASS/RIA on 23 June.
"About half of Crimea" lost power — a claim from occupation spokesperson Kryuchkov; the local supplier elsewhere cited "technical malfunctions."
The Russian sea-drone attack and Starlink fitting rest on a single Ukrainian official (Beskrestnov).

agree: Ukrainian strikes hit Crimea on 23 June.
Energy and rail infrastructure were affected.
Putin commented publicly; Crimea is experiencing power and fuel disruption.
contest: The scale of the blackout ("half" of Crimea).
Whether outages were caused by strikes or "technical malfunctions."
The extent of bridge destruction and the sea-drone account.

items: 1 · Single-source figure. The "half of Crimea without power" claim comes from occupation spokesperson Kryuchkov; "half" is imprecise and unverifiable, and the supplier's separate "technical malfunctions" line conflicts with the strike narrative.
2 · Unverified tallies. Ukrainian target counts ("60+ targets," specific systems destroyed) come from Ukrainian military channels and lack independent or imagery confirmation.
3 · One-sided account. The sea-drone attack and Starlink detail derive from one Ukrainian adviser, with no Russian acknowledgement.

stories.headline: Micron beats estimates and guides higher; shares jump after the Anthropic AI-memory deal
stories.summary: Micron reported fiscal Q3 revenue of about $41.5bn and adjusted EPS of $25.11, guided to roughly $50bn for the current quarter, and rose about 15% in after-hours trading, days after announcing a multi-year supply deal with Anthropic.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the Q3 results, guidance and after-hours move are corroborated across StockTitan, TheStreet, Investing.com and the Micron release; the earlier Anthropic deal and the week's sell-off are also confirmed

image.alt: A DDR SDRAM memory module on a white background
image.caption: The Micron–Anthropic agreement covers supply of memory products including DRAM and high-bandwidth memory. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 2.5

inline_image.alt: Rows of server racks in a data center server room
inline_image.caption: The supply deal targets data-centre memory and storage for AI training and inference workloads. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: Micron Technology reported fiscal third-quarter 2026 results after the U.S. market close on 24 June 2026 that exceeded analyst estimates, and issued guidance above expectations, sending its shares higher in after-hours trading. The company reported revenue of about $41.5 billion and adjusted earnings of $25.11 a share, with a non-GAAP gross margin of about 85%, according to its release and reporting by StockTitan and Investing.com.

For the current quarter, Micron guided to revenue of about $50.0 billion, plus or minus $1.0 billion, and adjusted earnings of about $31.00 a share, with a gross margin near 86%. The company said data-centre revenue exceeded $25 billion in the quarter. Micron shares rose about 15% in extended trading after the results, having closed roughly unchanged in the regular session, per TheStreet and Investing.com.

The results followed a strategic agreement that Micron and the AI developer Anthropic announced on 22 June 2026, covering memory and storage supply, joint technical work, use of Anthropic's Claude software inside Micron, and a Micron investment in Anthropic's Series H funding round. Financial terms of the agreement and the investment were not disclosed.

Under the supply component, the companies entered a multi-year memory and storage agreement spanning Micron's data-centre portfolio, including high-bandwidth memory, DRAM and solid-state drives, to support Anthropic's compute needs as it scales its Claude models. The two firms also said they will study how memory and storage subsystems perform across AI training and inference workloads.

The deal extends a relationship already disclosed in Anthropic's Series H round, which closed on 28 May 2026 at $65 billion raised and a $965 billion post-money valuation. That announcement named Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix among strategic infrastructure partners. Micron separately said it has deployed Claude internally for coding and agentic uses across engineering, manufacturing and enterprise functions.

The announcement came during a sharp sell-off in memory and AI-related equities. On 23 June 2026, South Korea's Kospi index triggered a market-wide circuit breaker, closing down 9.99%. SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics both fell more than 12% in the session. In the United States, the Nasdaq Composite declined about 2.2%, with chip and memory names among the larger decliners.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — Micron and Anthropic
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: 28 May
events.title: Anthropic closes Series H
events.detail: $65bn raised at a $965bn post-money valuation; Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix named as strategic partners.

events.label: 22 Jun
events.title: Micron–Anthropic agreement
events.detail: Multi-year memory and storage supply deal, joint technical work, Claude adoption and a Series H investment.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Memory-stock sell-off
events.detail: The Kospi triggers a circuit breaker and closes down 9.99%; SK Hynix and Samsung each fall more than 12%.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Micron Q3 earnings beat
events.detail: Micron reports ~$41.5bn revenue and $25.11 adjusted EPS, guides to ~$50bn; shares rise about 15% after hours.

article_blocks.text: "Our compute strategy depends on getting every layer of the stack right, and memory and storage are central to how efficiently we can train and serve Claude."
article_blocks.src: — Tom Brown, co-founder and chief compute officer, Anthropic, 22 June 2026

article_blocks.html: Reporting attributed the share-price falls to investor concern over the pace of hyperscaler AI-infrastructure spending, possible memory-pricing pressure as supply expands into 2027, interest-rate worries, and forced selling in leveraged products tied to semiconductor stocks. Micron's own shares had risen ahead of the broader sell-off; following the Anthropic announcement, Micron closed up 6.82% at $1,211.38, according to Benzinga.

The reported revenue and earnings were above the analyst consensus cited before the release by AlphaStreet, which had centred on roughly $34.5 billion in revenue and adjusted earnings near $19.7 to $20.0 a share. The after-hours gain reversed part of the week's decline in memory and AI-linked shares. Analysts said attention would turn to high-bandwidth-memory allocation, the durability of gross margins and demand into 2027; the after-hours move had not been confirmed by a regular-session close at the time of writing.

items: Fiscal Q3 2026 (24 June): revenue about $41.5bn, adjusted EPS $25.11, gross margin ~85% — above consensus.
Guidance for the current quarter: revenue about $50.0bn ±$1.0bn, adjusted EPS about $31.00; data-centre revenue topped $25bn.
Shares rose about 15% in after-hours trading after closing roughly flat in the regular session.
Micron–Anthropic strategic agreement announced 22 June 2026; financial terms not disclosed.
On 23 June the Kospi fell 9.99% and triggered a circuit breaker; SK Hynix and Samsung each fell more than 12%; the Nasdaq fell about 2.2%.

items.title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement
Anthropic raises $65B in Series H funding at $965B post-money valuation
Micron and Anthropic Strike Strategic AI Partnership
Micron Technology reports record results for the third quarter
Micron (MU) Q3 2026 earnings call updates
Micron tops Q3 2026 estimates, shares jump

items.text: Micron reported fiscal Q3 2026 revenue ~$41.5bn and adjusted EPS $25.11, and guided to ~$50bn for the current quarter (StockTitan, TheStreet, Investing.com).
Micron shares rose about 15% in after-hours trading following the results (TheStreet, Investing.com).
Micron–Anthropic agreement announced 22 June 2026; spans supply, technical collaboration, Claude adoption and a Series H investment; terms not disclosed.
The after-hours share move had not been confirmed by a regular-session close; the size of Micron's Series H investment was not disclosed.

items: 1 · Deal value undisclosed. No dollar figure exists for the supply agreement or Micron's Series H stake; do not infer a value from the $65bn round total.

2 · Correlation, not causation. The 23 June memory-stock sell-off is concurrent with, not caused by, the deal; sources tie the falls to broader AI-spend, pricing and rate concerns. Session attribution of Micron's own move varies slightly by outlet.

stories.headline: Study identifies why H5N1 infects dairy cows' udders rather than their lungs
stories.summary: University of Pittsburgh researchers found H5N1 binds N-linked sialic acid receptors that are abundant in cow udder tissue but scarce in their airways.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the journal, lead author and receptor mechanism are reported consistently by the University of Pittsburgh release, EurekAlert, ScienceDaily and Phys.org

image.alt: Black-and-white Holstein dairy cows in a barn
image.caption: Holstein dairy cattle; the study explains why H5N1 caused udder infections rather than respiratory illness in cows. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA

inline_image.alt: Colorized transmission electron micrograph of avian influenza A (H5N1) virus particles
inline_image.caption: A colorized electron micrograph of avian influenza A (H5N1) virus particles. Photo: CDC / Wikimedia Commons · public domain

article_blocks.html: Researchers at the University of Pittsburgh School of Public Health have reported a mechanistic explanation for why H5N1 avian influenza infects dairy cows' udders rather than their lungs. The study was published in the journal Science Advances on 19 June 2026.

When H5N1 began infecting U.S. dairy cattle in 2024, the disease looked different from the respiratory illness the virus causes in other mammals: it produced severe infection in the cows' udders and largely spared the lungs, which made it difficult for veterinarians to recognise at first.

Using binding experiments, staining methods and high-resolution imaging, the team found that not all of the cows' sialic acid receptors behaved the same way. Only a subtype known as N-linked sialic acid receptors could bind H5N1. Those receptors were nearly absent in the cows' airway tissue but widespread in udder tissue.

The lead author, Suresh Kuchipudi, chair of infectious diseases and microbiology at the school, said the finding helps explain the unusual pathology. "When the real culprit turned out to be bird flu, everyone in the field was caught completely by surprise," he said.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — H5N1 in cattle
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: Mar 2024
events.title: H5N1 detected in US dairy cattle
events.detail: Bird flu is confirmed in dairy herds, with infections concentrated in the udders.

events.title: Spread across herds
events.detail: The virus spreads among U.S. dairy cattle, with shedding into milk.

events.label: 19 Jun
events.title: Receptor study published
events.detail: University of Pittsburgh researchers report the receptor mechanism in Science Advances.

article_blocks.text: "We can preemptively screen different species and different tissues within them for susceptibility."
article_blocks.src: — Suresh Kuchipudi, University of Pittsburgh, 19 June 2026

article_blocks.html: Kuchipudi said the receptor distribution makes the udder "a perfect breeding ground for the virus," and noted that "if a cow is infected, it sheds a lot of virus into the milk." Pasteurisation inactivates the virus, and health agencies have said the commercial milk supply remains safe.

Why it matters: the researchers said the approach provides a framework other scientists can use to predict not just whether H5N1 can jump to new hosts, but how, by screening particular tissues for the receptors the virus needs. H5N1 now affects more than 100 bird and mammal species globally.

What comes next: the work identifies a binding mechanism rather than a treatment or vaccine. The authors framed it as a tool for surveillance and risk assessment; how the receptor pattern translates to transmission risk in other species remains to be tested.

items: Published in Science Advances on 19 June 2026 by University of Pittsburgh School of Public Health researchers.
H5N1 binds only N-linked sialic acid receptors, which are abundant in udder tissue but nearly absent in cow airways.
This explains why the virus caused udder infections (mastitis) rather than respiratory illness in cattle.
H5N1 was first confirmed in U.S. dairy cattle in 2024 and now affects more than 100 bird and mammal species globally.
Lead author: Suresh Kuchipudi, chair of infectious diseases and microbiology.

items.title: How H5N1 bird flu hid unrecognized for weeks in dairy cattle
Scientists solved how H5N1 bird flu hid in dairy cows
How H5N1 bird flu hid unrecognized for weeks in dairy cattle
How H5N1 bird flu hid unrecognized for weeks in dairy cattle

items.text: The study was published in Science Advances on 19 June 2026 by University of Pittsburgh researchers led by Suresh Kuchipudi.
H5N1 binds N-linked sialic acid receptors abundant in udder tissue and scarce in cow airways (university release; ScienceDaily; Phys.org).
How the receptor pattern translates to transmission risk in other species is a stated implication, not a demonstrated outcome.

items: 1 · Mechanism, not therapy. The study explains tissue tropism; it does not establish a treatment, vaccine or change to current control measures.
2 · Lab finding vs in-vivo. Receptor mapping and binding assays identify a plausible mechanism; predictive use across species remains to be validated.

stories.headline: SETI radio search of interstellar object 3I/ATLAS finds no technosignatures
stories.summary: A SETI Institute-led scan of the interstellar object 3I/ATLAS detected no artificial radio signals, with all candidate detections attributed to human-made interference.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — a peer-reviewed paper plus a separate Breakthrough Listen study are corroborated by Phys.org, Space.com and SETI Berkeley; frequencies, dates and power limits cross-checked

image.alt: Multiple white radio dishes of the Allen Telescope Array pointed at the sky
image.caption: The Allen Telescope Array at Hat Creek Radio Observatory, California, used by the SETI Institute to scan 3I/ATLAS. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0

inline_image.alt: Telescope image of interstellar object 3I/ATLAS as a faint point of light against background stars
inline_image.caption: Interstellar object 3I/ATLAS imaged by the International Gemini Observatory in 2025. Photo: NOIRLab/NSF/AURA · CC BY 4.0

article_blocks.html: Researchers searching the interstellar object 3I/ATLAS for radio signals that could indicate extraterrestrial technology have reported a null result, finding no transmissions beyond known sources of human-made interference. The object remains, by scientific consensus, a natural comet.

The principal search was carried out with the Allen Telescope Array, a 42-dish radio interferometer at the Hat Creek Radio Observatory in northern California, operated by the SETI Institute. The team observed 3I/ATLAS for a total of 7.25 hours across five sessions in July 2025, shortly after the object's discovery, scanning frequencies from 1 to 9 gigahertz for narrowband signals of the kind a transmitter might produce.

The search initially flagged nearly 74 million narrowband "hits." After filtering for radio-frequency interference and applying localisation checks, about 211 candidates remained for visual inspection. None were judged worthy of follow-up, and all were consistent with human-generated interference. The study, led by Sofia Z. Sheikh of the SETI Institute, was published in The Astronomical Journal in June 2026.

The null result allowed the team to place upper limits on any radio transmitter operating on or near the object, ruling out narrowband transmitters with an effective radiated power of roughly 10 to 110 watts — comparable to common household electronics.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — interstellar visitors
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.

events.label: Oct 2017
events.title: First interstellar object
events.detail: 1I/ʻOumuamua is discovered passing through the Solar System.

events.label: 1 Jul 2025
events.title: 3I/ATLAS discovered
events.detail: Found by the ATLAS survey in Chile; the third confirmed interstellar object.

events.label: 18 Dec 2025
events.title: Green Bank observations
events.detail: Breakthrough Listen scans near the object's closest approach, ~1–12 GHz.

events.label: Jun 2026
events.title: Results published
events.detail: The Allen Telescope Array null result is published in The Astronomical Journal.

article_blocks.text: "There is currently no evidence to suggest that interstellar objects are anything other than natural astrophysical objects."
article_blocks.src: — Breakthrough Listen study authors, Green Bank observations of 3I/ATLAS, December 2025

article_blocks.html: A separate, more sensitive campaign was conducted by the Breakthrough Listen program using the 100-metre Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope in West Virginia, on 18 December 2025, less than a day before the object's closest approach to Earth, across roughly 1 to 12 gigahertz. Those data found no continuous-wave transmitters above about 0.1 watts at the object's location; of more than 471,000 initial candidate signals, the nine remaining events were all attributed to interference.

3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar object to pass through the Solar System, after 1I/ʻOumuamua in 2017 and 2I/Borisov in 2019. It was discovered on 1 July 2025 and follows a highly eccentric, unbound trajectory, indicating an origin outside the Solar System.

Harvard astrophysicist Avi Loeb has catalogued what he describes as anomalies in the object's behaviour and has discussed the possibility of an artificial origin, while also stating that 3I/ATLAS is "most likely a comet of natural origin." Other astronomers attribute the features to ordinary cometary processes; observations of dust, gas and carbon dioxide around the object support the prevailing view that it is a natural comet.

items: The Allen Telescope Array observed 3I/ATLAS for 7.25 hours over five sessions in July 2025, scanning 1–9 GHz.
About 74 million narrowband hits were flagged; none survived as credible technosignatures.
The search constrains any transmitter to roughly 10–110 W; the Green Bank scan ruled out transmitters above ~0.1 W.
Green Bank observed on 18 December 2025, under a day before the object's closest approach to Earth.
3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar object, discovered 1 July 2025; consensus holds it is a natural comet.

items.title: A Search for Radio Technosignatures from 3I/ATLAS with the Allen Telescope Array
Interstellar object 3I/ATLAS reveals no technosignatures in seven-hour radio scan
Hunt for technosignatures around interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS comes up empty
Breakthrough Listen observations of interstellar object 3I/ATLAS

items.text: The Allen Telescope Array scanned 3I/ATLAS for 7.25 hours over five July 2025 sessions across 1–9 GHz, led by Sofia Z. Sheikh.
No credible technosignatures were found; all candidates were attributed to radio-frequency interference.
3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar object, discovered 1 July 2025; consensus treats it as a natural comet.

items: 1 · Absence of evidence. A null radio result rules out transmitters above the stated power limits within the observed bands and times — not the existence of any conceivable signal.

2 · Natural vs artificial. The radio searches constrain technosignatures but do not by themselves establish the object's nature; the natural-comet conclusion rests chiefly on imaging and spectroscopy of its dust and gas.

stories.headline: World Cup: Scotland beaten 2-0 by Brazil and exit; England held by Ghana
stories.summary: Vinícius Júnior scored twice as Brazil beat Scotland 2-0 in Miami on 24 June, ending Scotland's bid for a first World Cup knockout place, a day after England were held 0-0 by Ghana.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — the Scotland 2-0 result and Vinícius Júnior's two goals are corroborated across FIFA, Fox Sports and wire reporting; the England and Colombia results are also confirmed

image.alt: Exterior panorama of Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida
image.caption: Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, the venue for Scotland v Brazil on 24 June 2026. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 2.0

inline_image.alt: Scotland defender Andrew Robertson in a national-team shirt
inline_image.caption: Scotland captain Andrew Robertson, pictured in 2019; Scotland seek a first World Cup knockout place against Brazil. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 3.0

article_blocks.html: Brazil beat Scotland 2-0 in their final Group C match at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida, on 24 June 2026, ending Scotland's attempt to reach the World Cup knockout stage for the first time. Vinícius Júnior scored both goals, in the 7th minute and in first-half stoppage time.

The result left Brazil top of Group C and eliminated Scotland, who finished on three points. Scotland have not advanced beyond the group stage in any of their World Cup appearances. Morocco played Haiti in the group's other final fixture.

A day earlier, England drew 0-0 with Ghana in Group L at Gillette Stadium in Foxborough, Massachusetts, on 23 June 2026, and did not confirm a place in the knockout stage. England had about 79% possession and 19 shots; Nico O'Reilly struck the crossbar and Harry Kane shot over from close range, both late in the second half, and Marc Guehi had a header cleared off the line in stoppage time.
The result left both sides on four points from two matches, with England ahead on goal difference. England play Panama and Ghana play Croatia in their final group fixtures.

In Group K, Colombia beat DR Congo 1-0 at Estadio Akron in Zapopan, Mexico, with a 76th-minute goal from the defender Daniel Munoz that deflected past goalkeeper Lionel Mpasi. The win, Munoz's second goal of the tournament, took Colombia to six points and a place in the round of 32 before their final group game against Portugal.

DR Congo, on one point, were left needing to beat Uzbekistan in their final match to advance. Also in Group K on 23 June, Portugal beat Uzbekistan 5-0 in Houston, with Cristiano Ronaldo scoring twice; the result left Portugal level on points with Colombia at the top of the group.

article_blocks.head: Timeline — 23–24 June fixtures
article_blocks.sub: Tap an event to expand. Spacing reflects the time between events.
article_blocks.note: Scotland v Brazil and Morocco v Haiti were upcoming at compile time.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: England 0-0 Ghana
events.detail: Group L, Foxborough; O'Reilly hit the bar, Kane missed the rebound.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Colombia 1-0 DR Congo
events.detail: Group K, Zapopan; Munoz scored in the 76th minute to reach the last 32.

events.label: 23 Jun
events.title: Portugal 5-0 Uzbekistan
events.detail: Group K, Houston; Ronaldo scored twice.

events.label: 24 Jun
events.title: Scotland 0-2 Brazil
events.detail: Group C, Miami Gardens; Vinícius Júnior scored twice as Scotland were eliminated.

article_blocks.text: "I think we've shown in my time in charge that we can be flexible with our system. Pick the right team, give them the right tactics and rely on the players on the pitch."

article_blocks.html: Scotland had reached the final round of matches with three points, from a 1-0 win over Haiti and a 1-0 defeat by Morocco. The 2-0 loss to Brazil left them third in Group C and outside the qualification places. Manager Steve Clarke's side had been seeking to progress from the group stage for the first time in their World Cup history.

The expanded 48-team format sends the top two from each group, plus the eight best third-placed teams, to a round of 32. Scotland's three points and goal difference left them below the line for a best-third-placed berth as other groups concluded.

items: Scotland 0-2 Brazil (24 June, Miami Gardens): Vinícius Júnior scored in the 7th minute and in first-half stoppage time.
Scotland finished Group C on three points and were eliminated, having never reached a World Cup knockout stage.
England 0-0 Ghana (23 June): England had about 79% possession and 19 shots; O'Reilly hit the bar and Kane missed from close range.
Colombia 1-0 DR Congo (23 June): Muñoz scored in the 76th minute; Colombia reached six points and the round of 32.
The 48-team format sends the top two from each group plus the eight best third-placed teams to a round of 32.

items.title: England 0-0 Ghana — Final Score
Colombia 1-0 DR Congo — Munoz strike sends Colombia into last 32
Scotland v Brazil — match centre
Scotland vs. Brazil — box score, 24 June 2026
Muñoz sends Colombia into World Cup knockouts

items.text: Brazil beat Scotland 2-0 on 24 June at Miami Gardens, with Vinícius Júnior scoring twice (FIFA, Fox Sports, wire reporting).
England drew 0-0 with Ghana on 23 June at Foxborough; O'Reilly hit the bar and Kane missed (ESPN, Al Jazeera, FIFA).
Colombia beat DR Congo 1-0 via a Muñoz goal in the 76th minute, reaching the round of 32 (Sky Sports, ESPN, Al Jazeera).
The Morocco v Haiti result and full final Group C standings were not confirmed at compile time.

items: 1 · Result, not projection. The 2-0 scoreline and Vinícius Júnior's two goals are reported as a final result; one low-credibility outlet's differing scoreline was set aside.
2 · Elimination basis. Scotland's exit rests on finishing third on three points with their goal difference below the best-third-placed line; the Morocco v Haiti result was still being confirmed.

overview.headline: Wall Street steadies on rotation out of tech; oil and gold fall, Micron jumps after the close
overview.summary: U.S. stocks were mixed at Wednesday's close as the Dow rose and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq slipped on a rotation out of chips; Brent and gold fell on easing Middle East tension, and Micron rose about 15% after hours on its results.

confidence.label: Verified
confidence.why: — US, German and Japanese closing figures are the 24 June close, corroborated across two or more outlets; the FTSE 100's small move and some European single-stock figures vary by source and are labelled

image.alt: Exterior of the New York Stock Exchange building on Wall Street
image.caption: The New York Stock Exchange; U.S. equities closed mixed on 24 June, with the Dow higher and the Nasdaq lower. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0
image.credit: Beyond My Ken via Wikimedia Commons

inline_image.alt: Interior of the Frankfurt Stock Exchange
inline_image.caption: The Frankfurt Stock Exchange, home of the DAX, which closed about 1% lower on 24 June. Photo: Wikimedia Commons · CC BY-SA 4.0

article_blocks.html: U.S. equities closed mixed on Wednesday 24 June as investors rotated out of technology and AI-linked names into consumer and healthcare sectors. The S&P 500 eased 0.10% to 7,358.22 and the Nasdaq Composite slipped 0.43% to about 25,476.60, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.35% to 51,848.90 and the Russell 2000 added about 0.37% (all 24 June close).

The week's memory-chip-led decline steadied rather than deepening. After the close, Micron Technology rose about 15% in after-hours trading after reporting fiscal third-quarter revenue of roughly $41.5 billion and adjusted earnings of $25.11 a share, both above analyst estimates, and guiding the current quarter to about $50 billion. The results eased some concern about the pace of AI-infrastructure spending.

In Asia, Japan's Nikkei 225 closed down 0.88% at 69,174.97, while the Shanghai Composite ended little changed, up about 0.11% near 4,111 (24 June). In Europe, Germany's DAX closed down about 0.98% at 24,893.58 and the pan-regional Stoxx 600 finished modestly lower. London's FTSE 100 was little changed, closing around 10,429, with sources placing it between a small gain and a 0.09% fall; the property developer Segro rose sharply after a takeover approach.

Commodities fell as Middle East tensions eased. Brent crude dropped about 3.5% to around $75.5 a barrel and WTI fell roughly 3–4% to near $71, around their lowest in months, after Washington's 60-day arrangement on Iranian oil and as Strait of Hormuz traffic recovered. Spot gold fell about 3% to around $3,970–4,000 an ounce as the dollar index reached a 13-month high.

The macro backdrop remains a Fed leaning toward higher rates. At its 16–17 June meeting the FOMC held its benchmark rate at 3.5%–3.75% unanimously, dropped language biased toward cuts, and lifted its end-2026 median projection to about 3.8% from 3.4%, citing energy-driven inflation. On 24 June, Germany's Ifo business-climate index, U.S. new-home-sales data and weekly EIA crude inventories were among the releases.

What's next: after Micron's results, attention turns to the Federal Reserve's bank stress-test results on 25 June and the U.S. core PCE price index — the Fed's preferred inflation gauge — on 26 June. Investors said they would watch whether the rotation out of chips persists and how memory pricing and high-bandwidth-memory demand develop into 2027. Micron's after-hours move had not been confirmed by a regular-session close at the time of writing.

items: 24 June close: S&P 500 7,358.22 (-0.10%), Nasdaq ~25,476.60 (-0.43%), Dow 51,848.90 (+0.35%), Russell 2000 about +0.37%.
Micron rose about 15% after the close on a Q3 beat (~$41.5bn revenue, $25.11 adj. EPS) and ~$50bn guidance.
Nikkei 225 69,174.97 (-0.88%); DAX 24,893.58 (-0.98%); FTSE 100 little changed near 10,429 (24 June close).
Brent ~$75.5 (about -3.5%), WTI ~$71; gold ~$3,970–4,000/oz (about -3%) as Middle East tension eased; the dollar hit a 13-month high.
The Fed held at 3.5%–3.75% on 17 June; the median end-2026 projection was raised to about 3.8%.
Ahead: Fed bank stress-test results (25 June) and U.S. core PCE (26 June).

items: 1 · Previous close, pre-open. Compiled early on 25 June before markets opened; U.S., German and Japanese figures are the 24 June close (the latest completed session), and the Micron move is after-hours, not yet confirmed by a regular-session close.
2 · FTSE 100 spread. Sources differ on London's close (a small gain versus a 0.09% fall); the index is reported as little changed near 10,429 rather than to a single decimal.
3 · Single decimals vs rounding. Index levels are rounded where two sources differ; commodity moves are given as approximate percentage changes.

movers.dateline: Previous close: 24 June · US single stocks: 24 June after-hours
movers.headline: Tech slips at Wednesday's close; Micron and Qualcomm rise after hours
movers.note_html: At compile time (early 25 June) cash markets had not yet opened, so the figures below are the previous session's 24 June close. U.S. single-stock movers are after-hours moves on earnings, not regular-session closes. In the regular session the Dow rose and the Nasdaq fell as money rotated out of chips into healthcare and consumer names. UK movers are the 24 June London close. European single-stock movers could not be corroborated to two independent sources (readings conflicted) and are shown at index level only; see the DAX in the strip.

groups.section: United States — after-hours movers on earnings (24 June)
groups.note_html: After-hours moves following quarterly results; both closed the regular session roughly flat. Figures are approximate and not yet confirmed by a regular-session close.

groups.section: United Kingdom — FTSE 100 movers (24 June close)
groups.note_html: Segro rose after a takeover approach; property names led the risers. Defence stocks and oil and mining shares (Shell, BP, Rio Tinto) were among the fallers as crude prices dropped. UK figures are from London market summaries (Proactive, Hargreaves Lansdown, LSE).

archive.title: UK provisionally records its hottest June day as red heat warnings hit western Europe
archive.summary: The Met Office said the UK reached about 36°C on 24 June, provisionally its highest June temperature on record, as red extreme-heat warnings covered Surrey and South-East England and France and Italy issued alerts.

archive.title: US and Iran agree Hormuz safe-passage line; IMO to evacuate 11,000 seafarers
archive.summary: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran agreed a communication line for safe shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and a Lebanon de-confliction cell, as the IMO announced an evacuation of more than 11,000 stranded seafarers.

archive.title: Trump cancels housing-bill signing, conditioning it on an elections bill
archive.summary: Hours before a planned ceremony, Trump said he would not sign the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act until Congress passes the SAVE America Act on voter registration.

archive.title: Appeals court lets Trump administration resume nationwide expedited removal
archive.summary: A divided D.C. Circuit panel vacated a lower-court order, allowing fast-track deportation without a hearing for people who cannot prove two years' continuous US presence.

archive.title: Ukraine strikes occupied Crimea; occupation officials report widespread power loss
archive.summary: Ukraine said it destroyed a railway bridge over the North Crimean Canal and hit energy and fuel sites; occupation officials reported widespread blackouts and two people injured in Sevastopol.

archive.title: Micron beats estimates and guides higher; shares jump after the Anthropic AI-memory deal
archive.summary: Micron reported fiscal Q3 revenue of about $41.5bn and adjusted EPS of $25.11, guided to roughly $50bn for the current quarter, and rose about 15% after the close.

archive.title: Study identifies why H5N1 infects dairy cows' udders rather than their lungs
archive.summary: University of Pittsburgh researchers found H5N1 binds N-linked sialic acid receptors abundant in cow udder tissue but scarce in their airways; published in Science Advances.

archive.title: SETI radio search of interstellar object 3I/ATLAS finds no technosignatures
archive.summary: A SETI Institute-led scan detected no artificial radio signals from 3I/ATLAS, with all candidate detections attributed to human-made interference.

archive.title: World Cup: Scotland beaten 2-0 by Brazil and exit; England held by Ghana
archive.summary: Vinícius Júnior scored twice as Brazil beat Scotland 2-0 in Miami on 24 June, ending Scotland's bid for a first World Cup knockout place, a day after England were held 0-0 by Ghana.

archive.title: Wall Street steadies on rotation out of tech; oil and gold fall, Micron jumps after the close
archive.summary: U.S. stocks were mixed at Wednesday's close as the Dow rose and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq slipped; Brent and gold fell on easing Middle East tension and Micron rose about 15% after hours.
